headline: A 5-year bottom-up revenue & EBITDA model.
subhead: Twenty-seven addressable markets. Seven revenue streams. Base, bull, and bear scenarios across the full BiRAGAS addressable surface.
narration: BiRAGAS Financials. A five-year bottom-up revenue and EBITDA model. Twenty-seven addressable markets. Seven revenue streams. Base, bull, and bear scenarios across the full BiRAGAS addressable surface.

headline: A bottom-up model with credible upside.
subhead: Bottom-up customer-driven projection across the entire addressable surface — built to be tested, not just believed.
narration: The opportunity in one slide. A bottom-up model with credible upside. Forty-three million dollars of EBITDA in twenty twenty-eight. Four hundred eighty-two million dollars in base-case twenty thirty revenue. Forty-one percent base-case twenty thirty EBITDA margin. Bottom-up customer-driven projection across the entire addressable surface — built to be tested, not just believed.

title: Executive Summary
headline: Twenty-seven sized markets. Three scenarios. One investor-grade financial story.
subhead: Top-line conclusion: base-case 2030 revenue of $482M with $198M of EBITDA (41% margin). 2028 inflection: revenue $168M, EBITDA $43M (25.6% margin). EBITDA-positive from 2027 onward. Bull case 2030: $814M revenue, $415M EBITDA. Bear case 2030: $255M revenue, $72M EBITDA.
narration: Executive summary. Twenty-seven sized markets. Three scenarios. One investor-grade financial story. Top-line conclusion. Base-case twenty thirty revenue of four hundred eighty-two million dollars with one hundred ninety-eight million of EBITDA, a forty-one percent margin. The twenty twenty-eight inflection point. Revenue one hundred sixty-eight million, EBITDA forty-three million, twenty-five point six percent margin. EBITDA-positive from twenty twenty-seven onward.

title: Methodology
headline: Bottom-up. Customer-driven. Not top-down market share.
subhead: Six customer segments × ASP per segment × growth curve. ASPs benchmarked against comparable contract values in 10-K filings of Recursion, Schrödinger, and AbCellera, discounted 15-20% to reflect BiRAGAS's earlier-stage commercial maturity. Operating-leverage curve modeled on mature enterprise-SaaS comparables.
narration: Methodology. The model is bottom-up. Customer-driven. Not top-down market share. Six customer segments, multiplied by average selling price per segment, multiplied by a customer growth curve through twenty thirty. ASPs are benchmarked against comparable contract values disclosed in public ten-K filings of Recursion, Schrödinger, and AbCellera — then discounted fifteen to twenty percent to reflect BiRAGAS's earlier-stage commercial maturity.

title: Three Categories of Market
headline: Foundational. Substrate. Disease-vertical.
subhead: BiRAGAS plugs into three categories of addressable market simultaneously — earning revenue from analysis platforms, RNA-substrate platforms, and per-disease drug-development programs.

pillars.d: AI drug discovery · Target ID & validation · Companion diagnostics. BiRAGAS competes here directly.
RNA-Seq R&D + RNA Therapeutics. BiRAGAS extends and adds analytic value to these markets.
Oncology, kidney, autoimmune, neuro, metabolic. BiRAGAS earns target-ID and stratification fees.

narration: Three categories of market. Foundational, substrate, and disease-vertical. BiRAGAS plugs into all three simultaneously — earning revenue from analysis platforms, RNA-substrate platforms, and per-disease drug-development programs. This diversification across three market categories is what makes the model resilient.

title: AI Drug Discovery
headline: BiRAGAS competes here directly.
subhead: The market BiRAGAS lives in. Most platforms are correlational. BiRAGAS is the upstream causal-inference layer the rest of the market needs but does not have.
narration: AI Drug Discovery. The market BiRAGAS lives in. Two point eight billion dollars today, fourteen point five billion by twenty thirty — a thirty-nine percent compound growth rate. Most platforms in this market do correlational target ID or molecular optimization downstream of target choice. BiRAGAS is the upstream causal-inference layer the rest of the market needs but does not have. Sources — Grand View Research, Markets and Markets, Precedence Research.

title: RNA Sequencing R&D
headline: The substrate BiRAGAS analyzes.
subhead: RNA-seq is the experimental modality every BiRAGAS analysis is built on top of. We extend the value of Illumina, Thermo Fisher, BGI, and Pacific Biosciences sequencing output — we do not compete with them.
narration: RNA Sequencing R&D. The substrate BiRAGAS analyzes. Nine point two billion today, twenty-seven point four billion by twenty thirty. The analysis-layer share of this market is approximately fifteen to twenty percent of total spend — which means a serviceable market of four to five billion dollars by twenty thirty just for analytics on existing sequencing output. BiRAGAS does not compete with Illumina or Thermo Fisher. We extend their products' value.

title: RNA Therapeutics
headline: mRNA, siRNA, ASOs. The decade's biggest causal-inference opportunity.
subhead: Every RNA-therapeutic program needs a validated target. The failure rate of correlational target ID in RNA drugs is higher than in small molecules — RNA depends on exquisite biological specificity. BiRAGAS-addressable share: 3-5% of program spend.
narration: RNA Therapeutics. mRNA, siRNA, ASOs. The decade's biggest causal-inference opportunity. Twenty-one point four billion today, fifty-two billion by twenty thirty. Every RNA-therapeutic program needs a validated target — and the failure rate of correlational target ID in RNA drugs is higher than in small molecules because RNA depends on exquisite biological specificity. BiRAGAS-addressable share is three to five percent of program spend, or approximately one point six billion by twenty thirty.

title: Target ID & Validation
headline: The narrowest, most direct definition of BiRAGAS-addressable spend.
subhead: The slice of pharma R&D budget allocated specifically to target identification, validation, and biomarker discovery — activities BiRAGAS replaces or augments end-to-end.
narration: Target Identification and Validation. The narrowest, most direct definition of BiRAGAS-addressable spend. The slice of pharma R&D budget allocated specifically to target identification, validation, and biomarker discovery — activities that BiRAGAS replaces or augments end-to-end. Four point eight billion today, nine point two billion by twenty thirty.

title: Companion Diagnostics
headline: Royalty-bearing motion.
subhead: BiRAGAS-discovered biomarkers can co-develop with sponsor drugs and earn tiered royalties on the launched diagnostic. First launch projected 2028; three cumulative launches contributing royalty by 2030.
narration: Companion Diagnostics. A royalty-bearing motion. Seven point six billion today, nineteen point three billion by twenty thirty. BiRAGAS-discovered biomarkers can co-develop with sponsor drugs and earn tiered royalties on the launched diagnostic. First launch projected for twenty twenty-eight. Three cumulative launches contributing royalty by twenty thirty.

title: Oncology — total
headline: The largest single addressable vertical.
subhead: BiRAGAS plugs into target ID + patient stratification across 7 sub-segments — every major one is biomarker-driven.
narration: Oncology — total. The largest single addressable vertical. Two hundred twenty billion today, three hundred ninety-six billion by twenty thirty, growing at twelve point five percent compound. BiRAGAS plugs into target ID and patient stratification across all seven oncology sub-segments — every major one is biomarker-driven. The FDA's evolving guidance toward mechanistic causal evidence raises the bar in BiRAGAS's favor.

title: Oncology — large markets
headline: $50B+ markets that BiRAGAS plugs into.
subhead: The three largest oncology sub-segments. All biomarker-stratified. All susceptible to causal sub-clustering BiRAGAS provides.

pillars.d: $28.5B → $50B · 11.9% CAGR. KRAS, EGFR, ALK stratification. Causal sub-clustering opportunity.
$32B → $52B · 10.3% CAGR. HR+/HER2/TNBC. Resistance-mechanism discovery.
$14.6B → $24B · 10.5% CAGR. MSI-H, RAS, BRAF. Emerging causal-network mapping.

narration: Oncology sub-segments — large markets. The three largest oncology sub-segments. NSCLC, breast, and colorectal — together one hundred twenty-six billion by twenty thirty. All biomarker-stratified. All susceptible to the causal sub-clustering BiRAGAS provides. NSCLC alone reaches fifty billion by twenty thirty.

title: Oncology — mid-size markets
headline: Prostate. Melanoma. Pancreatic.
subhead: Three sub-segments with active immunotherapy and targeted-therapy waves. Each has clear unmet need for causal stratification.

pillars.d: $13.1B → $22B · 10.8% CAGR. AR-pathway + DNA repair (PARP) sub-segments.
$8.5B → $14.5B · 11.3% CAGR. BRAF/MEK + IO resistance — BiRAGAS-suited.
$4.8B → $9.2B · 13.9% CAGR. High unmet need · KRAS-G12C/G12D wave starting.

narration: Oncology sub-segments — mid-size markets. Prostate, melanoma, and pancreatic. Three sub-segments with active immunotherapy and targeted-therapy waves. Prostate at twenty-two billion by twenty thirty. Melanoma at fourteen point five billion. Pancreatic at nine point two billion — high unmet need with the KRAS-G12C wave just beginning.

title: Hepatocellular Carcinoma
headline: TKI + IO combos. Causal subtyping early.
subhead: Smallest of the modeled oncology sub-segments, but highest CAGR. The field is in early stratification — BiRAGAS-natural target.
narration: Hepatocellular carcinoma. TKI plus immunotherapy combos. Causal subtyping is early in this field. Three point six billion today, seven point one billion by twenty thirty — highest growth rate among the modeled oncology sub-segments at fourteen point six percent. The field is in early stratification, making it a natural BiRAGAS target.

title: AML — Acute Myeloid Leukemia
headline: FLT3-ITD resistance. WDR4 driver. BiRAGAS-validated.
subhead: Mortality has remained unchanged for fifteen years. BiRAGAS has identified novel FLT3-resistance biomarkers and the WDR4 driver — peer-reviewed work in publication queue.
narration: AML — acute myeloid leukemia. Four point four billion today, seven point two billion by twenty thirty. Mortality has remained unchanged for fifteen years. BiRAGAS has identified novel FLT3-resistance biomarkers and the WDR4 driver — peer-reviewed work is in the publication queue. Multiple sub-segments — NPM1, IDH1, IDH2, TP53 — are addressable. Patient stratification is the dominant unmet need.

title: Diabetic Nephropathy
headline: Highest CAGR among landing verticals.
subhead: BiRAGAS deploys a subscription clinic model + companion diagnostic, with in-house cohort data already validated. The market is rapidly expanding behind SGLT2-inhibitor and finerenone tailwinds.
narration: Diabetic nephropathy. Highest CAGR among our landing verticals at twenty-six point two percent. Five point eight billion today, eighteen point six billion by twenty thirty. BiRAGAS deploys a subscription clinic model plus a companion diagnostic, with in-house cohort data already validated. The market is rapidly expanding behind the SGLT2-inhibitor and finerenone tailwinds.

title: IBD — Crohn's + Ulcerative Colitis
headline: Primary loss-of-response is the biggest unmet need in the field.
subhead: JAK / IL-23 / TNF response stratification is exactly what BiRAGAS's causal endotype modeling does. Conservative penetration target: 1.5% of SAM by Year-5.
narration: Inflammatory bowel disease. Crohn's plus ulcerative colitis. Twenty point four billion today, thirty-two billion by twenty thirty. Primary loss-of-response is the biggest unmet need in the field. JAK, IL-23, and TNF response stratification is exactly what BiRAGAS's causal endotype modeling does. Conservative penetration target — one point five percent of serviceable market by Year-5.

title: Connective Tissue Diseases
headline: Five autoimmune sub-markets — heterogeneous, underserved by current stratification.
subhead: The autoimmune category has highest heterogeneity — making causal endotyping highest-value. SLE is BiRAGAS-natural target.

pillars.d: $28B → $36B · 5.2% CAGR. Mature market — biomarker stratification still under-served.
$3B → $5.4B · 12.4% CAGR. Highly heterogeneous — BiRAGAS-natural causal endotype target.
$25.4B → $32.4B · 5.1% CAGR. Mature DMT market · disability-progression endotype opportunity.
$32.2B → $47B · 7.8% CAGR. IL-17 / IL-23 stratification. Causal modeling for treatment failure.
$2.8B → $5.6B · 14.9% CAGR. Scleroderma · Sjögren's · myositis · MCTD.

narration: Connective tissue diseases. Five autoimmune sub-markets — heterogeneous, and underserved by current stratification. Rheumatoid arthritis at thirty-six billion by twenty thirty. Lupus at five point four billion — highest heterogeneity in the category, making it a natural BiRAGAS target. Multiple sclerosis at thirty-two billion. Psoriasis and psoriatic arthritis at forty-seven billion. Plus orphan CTDs at five point six billion.

title: Neurodegeneration
headline: Post-Leqembi inflection in Alzheimer's. PD, ALS open behind it.
subhead: Alzheimer's is the third-highest CAGR in our model at 26.8 percent. PD and ALS are smaller but high-margin orphan adjacencies.

pillars.d: $7.5B → $24.5B · 26.8% CAGR. Post-Leqembi inflection. Biomarker stratification + responder ID.
$5.6B → $11.4B · 15.3% CAGR. LRRK2 / GBA genetic subtypes — causal stratification opportunity.
$0.9B → $1.9B · 16.1% CAGR. C9orf72 / SOD1 — orphan but high-margin.

narration: Neurodegeneration. Post-Leqembi inflection in Alzheimer's. Parkinson's disease and ALS open behind it. Alzheimer's at twenty-four point five billion by twenty thirty — the third-highest CAGR in our model at twenty-six point eight percent. Parkinson's at eleven point four billion. ALS at one point nine billion — orphan but high-margin.

title: Metabolic, Mental Health, Kidney-Other
headline: Three additional verticals compounding the addressable surface.
subhead: Obesity is the largest single-disease growth opportunity of the decade. Depression / TRD opens responder-stratification revenue. CKD-non-diabetic extends BiRAGAS's kidney beachhead.

pillars.d: $22B → $70B · 26% CAGR. GLP-1 wave. Patient-responder stratification + secondary metabolic-cluster targets.
$19.5B → $28.6B · 8.0% CAGR. TRD biomarker stratification emerging.
$3.8B → $9.2B · 19.3% CAGR. FSGS, IgA nephropathy, ADPKD. Extends BiRAGAS's DN beachhead.

narration: Metabolic, mental health, and kidney-other. Three additional verticals compounding the addressable surface. Obesity at seventy billion by twenty thirty — the largest single-disease growth opportunity of the decade, driven by GLP-1 agonists. Depression and treatment-resistant depression at twenty-eight point six billion. Non-diabetic chronic kidney disease at nine point two billion — extends BiRAGAS's kidney beachhead.

headline: Across all 27 markets — $1 trillion+ in 2030 TAM.
subhead: BiRAGAS's serviceable obtainable market (SOM) at Year-5 penetration assumptions is approximately $1.6 billion. Base-case 2030 revenue of $482M = 30% capture of SOM.
narration: Total addressable. Across all twenty-seven markets — one trillion fifty billion in twenty thirty TAM. BiRAGAS's serviceable obtainable market at Year-five penetration assumptions is approximately one point six billion dollars. Base-case twenty thirty revenue of four hundred eighty-two million is a thirty percent capture of SOM — aggressive but defensible given the platform's foundation-level moat and the accelerating FDA causal-evidence tailwind.

title: Seven Revenue Streams
headline: Seven concurrent revenue motions.
subhead: Six SaaS / licensing segments + co-development partnerships + companion-diagnostic royalties. Diversified by customer type, by contract length, and by revenue recognition timing.

pillars.d: Enterprise license, $1.80M avg/yr
Modular license, $0.70M/yr
Self-serve, $0.20M/yr
Platform + re-license rights, $1.00M/yr
Research-only license, $0.15M/yr
Federal contract, $0.25M/yr
$5M upfront + $38M milestones + royalty

narration: Seven revenue streams. Six SaaS or licensing segments — Big Pharma, Mid-cap Biotech, Small Biotech, CROs, Academic Medical Centers, and Government — plus co-development partnerships and companion-diagnostic royalties. Diversified by customer type, by contract length, and by revenue-recognition timing.

title: Stream 1 — Big Pharma Platform
headline: Enterprise license to the world's top 25 pharma sponsors.
subhead: Full platform access. Dedicated support. Per-program causal-inference runs. 9-12 month sales cycle, longest of any segment. By 2030: 30 customers (~120% of the top 25 includes a number of foreign and mid-tier names). This is the highest-ARR segment.
narration: Stream one. Big Pharma platform license. One point eight million dollars per customer per year. Enterprise license to the world's top twenty-five pharma sponsors. Full platform access. Dedicated support. Per-program causal-inference runs. Nine to twelve month sales cycle, longest of any segment. By twenty thirty — thirty customers.

title: Stream 2 — Mid-cap Biotech
headline: Modular license to $50M+ R&D biotechs.
subhead: Modular pricing — sponsors choose which BiRAGAS modules to access (causal DAG, MR, in-silico perturbation, etc.). 6 month sales cycle. By 2030: 130 customers — the largest single revenue segment by total dollars.
narration: Stream two. Mid-cap biotech modular license. Seven hundred thousand dollars per customer per year. License to companies with fifty million dollars plus in annual R&D budget. Modular — sponsors choose which BiRAGAS modules to access. Six month sales cycle. By twenty thirty — one hundred thirty customers, the largest single revenue segment by total dollars.

title: Stream 3 — Small Biotech SaaS
headline: Self-serve SaaS for Series A and B.
subhead: Lower per-customer support overhead. 3 month sales cycle. Highest churn risk segment (18% annually) but also fastest customer-acquisition velocity. By 2030: 230 customers.
narration: Stream three. Small biotech SaaS subscription. Two hundred thousand dollars per customer per year. Self-serve SaaS for Series A and B startups. Lighter feature set. Lower per-customer support overhead. Three month sales cycle — the fastest in our portfolio. Highest churn risk segment at eighteen percent annually, but also fastest customer-acquisition velocity. By twenty thirty — two hundred thirty customers.

title: Stream 4 — CRO White-label
headline: Tier-1 CROs re-license BiRAGAS modules to their clients.
subhead: Platform license with white-label rights. Effectively extends BiRAGAS's commercial reach via CRO sales channels. By 2030: 32 customers (~64% of the ~50 tier-1 CROs globally).
narration: Stream four. CRO white-label license. One million dollars per customer per year. Tier-one contract-research organizations sub-license BiRAGAS modules to their own clients. White-label rights available. Effectively extends BiRAGAS's commercial reach via CRO sales channels. By twenty thirty — thirty-two customers, about sixty-four percent of the fifty tier-one CROs globally.

title: Stream 5 — Academic Medical Centers
headline: Top-150 US AMCs + equivalent international.
subhead: Research-only license. Lower ARR per customer but the highest customer-count growth and the source of clinical-research collaborations that feed co-development pipeline. By 2030: 80 customers.
narration: Stream five. AMC research-tier license. One hundred fifty thousand dollars per customer per year. Top-one-hundred-fifty US academic medical centers and equivalent international research institutions. Research-only license — no commercial use. Lower ARR per customer, but the highest customer-count growth and the source of clinical-research collaborations that feed our co-development pipeline. By twenty thirty — eighty customers.

title: Stream 6 — Government / NIH
headline: Federally-funded labs and NIH-grant-attached pricing.
subhead: Government and disease-foundation contracts. NIH grant-attached pricing model. Long, stable contract durations (3-5 years typical). By 2030: 60 customers.
narration: Stream six. Government and NIH contract. Two hundred fifty thousand dollars per customer per year. Federally-funded labs, consortia, and disease foundations. NIH grant-attached pricing. Long, stable contract durations — typically three to five years. By twenty thirty — sixty customers.

title: Stream 7a — Co-Development
headline: $5M upfront. $38M milestones. 3-7% royalty.
subhead: Strategic arrangements with named pharma partners where BiRAGAS operates upstream target discovery for a specific indication. The asymmetric-upside motion.

steps.d: $8M when first target moves to IND-enabling studies.
$18M on Phase 1 readout or partner option exercise.
3-7% of partner's net sales on the resulting therapy.

narration: Stream seven-A. Co-development partnerships. Five million dollars upfront per partnership. Thirty-eight million in milestones over years two through four. Plus a three to seven percent royalty on the partner's net sales on the resulting therapy. Strategic arrangements with named pharma partners where BiRAGAS operates upstream target discovery for a specific indication. The asymmetric-upside motion.

title: Stream 7b — Companion Diagnostic Royalties
headline: $1.2M → $6M / year per launch, ramping over 3 years.
subhead: Royalties on BiRAGAS-discovered companion diagnostics approved alongside sponsor drugs. First launch projected 2028 (DN companion-Dx, already engaged with FDA via Pre-Sub). Year-of-launch $1.2M, +1 year $3.6M, +2 year $6M steady state.
narration: Stream seven-B. Companion diagnostic royalties. One point two million dollars in year of launch, three point six million in year plus one, six million in year plus two — steady-state ramp. Per launched companion diagnostic. First launch projected for twenty twenty-eight — the DN companion-Dx, already engaged with FDA via Pre-Submission. Three cumulative launches contributing royalty by twenty thirty.

title: Customer Cohorts — Base Case
headline: Bottom-up — every customer count is testable against a named pipeline.
subhead: Customer growth ramps fastest in mid-cap biotech (lowest sales cycle) and slowest in big pharma (longest cycle). Diversification across segments holds from Year 1 forward.
narration: Customer cohorts, base case. End-of-year customer counts. Bottom-up — every customer count is testable against a named pipeline. Twenty twenty-six — forty-seven customers. Twenty twenty-seven — one hundred fourteen. Twenty twenty-eight — two hundred twenty-nine. Twenty twenty-nine — three hundred sixty-nine. Twenty thirty — five hundred sixty-two.

title: 2028 — The Inflection Year
headline: 2028. EBITDA $43M. Margin 25.6%.
subhead: The year BiRAGAS crosses from growth-stage scale into mature-SaaS profitability. The 2028 P&L is the test the model must pass — and the inflection investors should track.
narration: The twenty twenty-eight inflection year. The year BiRAGAS crosses from growth-stage scale into mature-SaaS profitability. EBITDA forty-three million dollars. Twenty-five point six percent margin. This is the test the model must pass — and the inflection investors should track.

title: 5-Year P&L — Base Case
headline: Base case 5-year income statement.
subhead: From SaaS, co-dev, and CDx revenue through gross profit and OpEx to EBITDA. Numbers tie back to the workbook's P&L Base sheet.
narration: Five-year P&L — base case. Total revenue ramps from twenty-six million in twenty twenty-six to four hundred eighty-two million in twenty thirty. Gross margin expands from sixty percent to eighty percent. EBITDA — negative four million in twenty twenty-six, positive thirteen million in twenty twenty-seven, forty-three million in twenty twenty-eight, then one hundred two million in twenty twenty-nine, and one hundred ninety-eight million in twenty thirty. EBITDA margin expands from negative fifteen to forty-one percent.

title: 5-Year P&L — Bull Case
headline: Bull case — upside surface.
subhead: Reflects faster customer ramp + faster co-dev deal flow + additional scale economies. Same gross margin curve, lower OpEx ratios.
narration: Bull case. Upside surface. Reflects faster customer ramp plus faster co-development deal flow plus additional scale economies. Same gross-margin curve, lower OpEx ratios. Total revenue scales from forty-five million in twenty twenty-six to eight hundred fourteen million in twenty thirty. EBITDA reaches four hundred fifteen million — a fifty-one percent margin.

title: 5-Year P&L — Bear Case
headline: Bear case — downside surface.
subhead: Reflects slower customer ramp, slower co-dev deal flow, OpEx ratio takes 12 extra months to compress. Still EBITDA-positive by 2028.
narration: Bear case. Downside surface. Reflects slower customer ramp, slower co-development deal flow, and OpEx ratios taking an additional twelve months to compress. Still EBITDA-positive by twenty twenty-eight. Total revenue ramps from thirteen million in twenty twenty-six to two hundred fifty-five million in twenty thirty. EBITDA reaches seventy-two million by twenty thirty — twenty-eight percent margin.

headline: Three scenarios. All EBITDA-positive by 2028.
subhead: Even bear case crosses to positive EBITDA in 2028. Even bull case stays grounded in customer-count reality.
narration: Scenario comparison — twenty thirty. Three scenarios. All EBITDA-positive by twenty twenty-eight. Bear case — two hundred fifty-five million revenue, twenty-eight percent EBITDA margin. Base case — four hundred eighty-two million revenue, forty-one percent margin. Bull case — eight hundred fourteen million revenue, fifty-one percent margin.

title: Operating Leverage
kicker: OpEx ratios decline as platform scales
headline: From 235% OpEx ratio to 56%.
subhead: Classic SaaS operating leverage curve — fixed costs spread over a larger revenue base. Year-1 burn is the investment; Year-5 mature operating margins are the payoff.
narration: Operating leverage. From two hundred thirty-five percent OpEx ratio in the first year to fifty-six percent by year five — a classic SaaS operating-leverage curve. Fixed costs spread over a larger revenue base as the platform scales. Year-one burn is the investment. Year-five mature operating margins are the payoff.

title: Unit Economics by Segment
headline: Every segment is unit-economically profitable.
subhead: LTV/CAC ratios benchmarked conservatively. Big-pharma 56× reflects long contracts and low churn. Small biotech 11.5× reflects highest churn but fastest acquisition velocity.
narration: Unit economics by segment. Every segment is unit-economically profitable. Big pharma — LTV over CAC fifty-six times, reflecting long contracts and low churn. Mid-cap biotech twenty-five times. Small biotech eleven and a half times — reflects highest churn but fastest acquisition velocity. CROs thirty-six times. AMCs fifteen times. Government nineteen times. All payback periods under six months.

title: Sensitivity — 2030 Revenue
headline: Penetration × pricing heatmap.
subhead: If customer count comes in at 80% of base and ASPs at 80% of base, 2030 revenue still lands at $309M. The model is not knife-edge.
narration: Sensitivity. Twenty thirty revenue under penetration times pricing combinations. If customer count comes in at eighty percent of base and ASPs at eighty percent of base, twenty thirty revenue still lands at three hundred nine million dollars. The model is not knife-edge. Even at sixty percent penetration with full pricing, revenue is two hundred fifty-five million — still cash-flow positive.

title: Path to Profitability
headline: Negative in 2026. Positive from 2027. $43M EBITDA in 2028.
subhead: The full trajectory of EBITDA from launch through scale.

steps.d: $(4M) EBITDA · -15% margin. Platform investment, first customers.
+$13M · +17%. First co-dev wave pays out. EBITDA-positive.
+$102M · +34%. Second co-dev cohort matures. Compound margin expansion.

narration: Path to profitability. EBITDA trajectory. Twenty twenty-six — investment year. Negative four million. Negative fifteen percent margin. Platform investment, first customers. Twenty twenty-seven — crossover year. Positive thirteen million. Seventeen percent margin. First co-dev wave pays out. Twenty twenty-eight — the inflection year. Forty-three million. Twenty-five point six percent margin. Mature-SaaS profitability arrives. Twenty twenty-nine — scale. One hundred two million. Thirty-four percent. Twenty thirty — operating leverage maturity. One hundred ninety-eight million. Forty-one percent.

title: Risks & Mitigations
headline: What could go wrong — and what we've already done about it.
subhead: Every material model risk has been stress-tested against a named mitigation. No single risk breaks the 2028 EBITDA threshold.

pillars.d: 48% of 2030 base revenue is non-big-pharma SaaS. -18 month delay = -11% revenue impact.
Value depends on FDA accepting causal evidence, not mandating it. DN Pre-Sub already engaged.
3-4 year architectural catch-up cost. Foundation gap, not feature gap.
CDx contributes only 0.7% of 2028 revenue. $43M 2028 EBITDA holds within rounding.
No single customer >8% of revenue. Year-1 diversification baked in.
BiRAGAS 3× more compute-efficient than Recursion. 30% COGS still gives $35M 2028 EBITDA.

narration: Risks and mitigations. Six identified risks — all mitigated. Every material model risk has been stress-tested against a named mitigation. No single risk breaks the forty-three million dollar twenty twenty-eight EBITDA threshold.

title: Risk 1 — Slower Big Pharma Sales
headline: 48% of 2030 base revenue is non-big-pharma SaaS.
subhead: Mid-cap biotech, small biotech, CROs, AMCs, and government segments collectively deliver $230M of base-case 2030 revenue independent of big-pharma. If big-pharma sales lag by 18 months and customer count drops 50%, base-case 2030 revenue is reduced by only ~11%.
narration: Risk one. Slower-than-expected enterprise sales cycle in big pharma. Mitigation. Forty-eight percent of base-case twenty thirty revenue comes from non-big-pharma SaaS segments. Mid-cap biotech, small biotech, CROs, AMCs, and government segments collectively deliver two hundred thirty million of base-case twenty thirty revenue independent of big pharma. Even if big-pharma sales lag by eighteen months and customer count drops fifty percent, base-case twenty thirty revenue is reduced by only eleven percent.

title: Risk 2 — FDA Timeline Slip
headline: Value depends on FDA accepting causal evidence — not mandating it.
subhead: The DN companion-diagnostic Pre-Submission is already engaged. An accept-as-equivalent regulatory posture is sufficient for the base case — even if FDA delays mandating mechanistic evidence until 2028, BiRAGAS-discovered claims still qualify under existing 21 CFR 809.40.
narration: Risk two. FDA causal-evidence guidance does not advance on schedule. Mitigation. BiRAGAS's value proposition does not depend on FDA mandating causal evidence — it depends on FDA accepting causal evidence in dossiers. The diabetic nephropathy companion-diagnostic Pre-Submission is already engaged. An accept-as-equivalent regulatory posture is sufficient for the base case.

title: Risk 3 — Competitive Entry
headline: 3-4 year architectural catch-up cost — even with unlimited engineering budget.
subhead: BiRAGAS's foundation gap is structural, not feature-level. Replicating the unified causal DAG with Mendelian randomization + CRISPR perturbation + SIGNOR + causal calculus requires re-deriving the integration logic across four streams. Even an aggressive competitor entering in 2027 cannot match installed-base pricing before 2030.
narration: Risk three. Larger AI drug discovery competitors enter causal target ID. Mitigation. Documented three to four year architectural catch-up cost. BiRAGAS's foundation gap is structural, not feature-level. To match BiRAGAS, a competitor would need three to four years of rearchitecture from the substrate up — even with unlimited engineering budget.

title: Risk 4 — CDx Royalty Slip
headline: CDx contribution to 2028 base revenue: only 0.7%.
subhead: Pulling the first CDx launch back to 2029 reduces 2028 EBITDA by less than $1M; the $43M 2028 EBITDA holds within rounding. The CDx upside is a 2029-2030 story, not a 2028 dependency.
narration: Risk four. Companion diagnostic royalty timeline slips past twenty twenty-eight. Mitigation. CDx contribution to base-case twenty twenty-eight revenue is only one point two million dollars — zero point seven percent of total. Pulling the first CDx launch back to twenty twenty-nine reduces twenty twenty-eight EBITDA by less than one million; the forty-three million dollar twenty twenty-eight EBITDA still holds within rounding.

title: Risk 5 — Customer Concentration
headline: No single customer exceeds 8% of revenue in any year.
subhead: Year-1 diversification is baked in: 3 big pharma + 10 mid-cap + 18 small biotech + 3 CROs + 8 AMCs + 5 government = 47 customers in 2026. Concentration risk does not materialize even in the bear case.
narration: Risk five. Customer concentration risk if big-pharma contracts dominate early. Mitigation. Customer-segment diversification is baked into twenty twenty-six — three big pharma plus ten mid-cap plus eighteen small biotech plus three CROs plus eight AMCs plus five government — forty-seven customers in year one. No single customer exceeds eight percent of revenue in any year through twenty thirty.

title: Risk 6 — Compute Cost Scaling
headline: BiRAGAS is 3× more compute-efficient per output than Recursion.
subhead: COGS modeled at 25% in 2028 — conservative ceiling. Recursion 10-K shows 32% COGS at $50M revenue. BiRAGAS's lower-touch causal inference workload runs ~3× more compute-efficient. If COGS rises to 30%, 2028 EBITDA still lands at ~$35M.
narration: Risk six. Margins compress because compute costs scale faster than revenue. Mitigation. COGS is modeled at twenty-five percent in twenty twenty-eight — a conservative ceiling. Recursion's ten-K shows thirty-two percent COGS at fifty million revenue. BiRAGAS's lower-touch causal inference workload runs three times more compute-efficient per output. If COGS rises to thirty percent, twenty twenty-eight EBITDA still lands at approximately thirty-five million dollars — the directional case still holds.

title: The Round
headline: SAFE · $40M cap · 20% disc · Refund Right.
subhead: Standard Y Combinator post-money SAFE structure with one critical modification: a milestone-gated 12-month Refund Right rider. If BiRAGAS misses defined operating milestones, the investor elects a refund.

round.v: Rolling · Q3 2026 first close · Q3 2027 final
Both included via Anchor Investor Side Letter

narration: The Round. SAFE with twenty percent discount, forty-million-dollar post-money cap, and the Refund Right rider. The standard YC post-money SAFE structure with one critical modification — a milestone-gated twelve-month Refund Right rider. If BiRAGAS misses defined operating milestones in twelve months, the investor elects a refund of the purchase amount. If we hit them, the SAFE converts at the Series A at a twenty percent discount.

title: The Ask
headline: BiRAGAS is infrastructure — with downside protection and a fully modeled path to $43M of 2028 EBITDA.
subhead: Capital for the path to mature-SaaS profitability. If we hit the gates, you convert into Series A at a 20% discount on a fully-modeled ARR ramp. If we don't, you elect a refund. Either way, the asymmetry runs in your favor.
narration: The Ask. BiRAGAS is infrastructure — with downside protection and a fully modeled path to forty-three million dollars of twenty twenty-eight EBITDA. Capital for the path to mature-SaaS profitability. If we hit the gates, you convert into Series A at a twenty percent discount on a fully-modeled ARR ramp. If we don't, you elect a refund. Either way, the asymmetry runs in your favor.

kicker: From the trillion to the operating story
headline: Twenty-seven markets. Seven streams. Three scenarios. One coherent story.
subhead: A $1.05 trillion 2030 TAM. $1.6 billion serviceable obtainable market. $482M base-case 2030 revenue. $198M of 2030 EBITDA. All built bottom-up from named customer pipelines and benchmarked ASPs. That's BiRAGAS Financials.
narration: From the trillion to the operating story. Twenty-seven markets. Seven streams. Three scenarios. One coherent story. A one trillion dollar twenty thirty TAM. One point six billion dollar serviceable obtainable market. Four hundred eighty-two million dollar base-case twenty thirty revenue. One hundred ninety-eight million dollar EBITDA. All built bottom-up from named customer pipelines and benchmarked ASPs. That's BiRAGAS Financials.
